Applied Machine Learning  ·  Credit Risk  ·  Selection Bias

Who Did We Leave Out?

Credit models learn from borrowers who were approved. But the people who were turned down are the ones where the predictions matter most. This is what happens when you take that seriously.

Lacy Dove  ·  Michael Beyer  ·  Aryan Sanan  ·  Harelle Keli
Reject Inference Survival Analysis Fairness Home Credit Dataset

Every credit model you've ever trusted was trained on the people it never had to worry about. Here's what happens when you test it on everyone else.

This project traces the problem of selection bias through five phases of analysis, using 307,511 Home Credit loan applications enriched with a synthetic rejection population. We asked three questions: how bad is the selection distortion, can three correction methods fix it, and does any of this change how the model treats different groups of people? The answers are specific, and they matter for anyone who deploys or regulates credit models.

01
Exploratory Analysis

Understanding the Borrowers

Before fitting a single model, we mapped the structure of default risk across 307,511 loan applications. Three patterns dominated everything that followed.

External scores dominate

The composite external score we built from three bureau sources creates a default rate gradient that no other variable matches. Bottom-quintile borrowers default at roughly 19–21%, while top-quintile borrowers come in at 2–3%. That spread shapes every downstream modeling decision.

Default rate by education level. Lower secondary borrowers default at 10.9%, while higher education drops to 5.4%. Academic degree holders (1.8%) represent a small group where sample size limits interpretation.
Age × External Score. Both factors push in the same direction — additively. Young borrowers with low external scores reach 19–21% default; older borrowers with high scores drop below 2%.
Age × Credit-to-Income. Debt burden amplifies baseline risk most sharply among younger borrowers. The interaction flattens considerably past age 50.
Education × External Score. Even within education groups, external scores create a strong gradient. Education reduces baseline risk but cannot fully offset a poor external score.
Housing × Credit-to-Income. Renters (12.3%) remain consistently higher risk across all debt levels versus house/apartment owners (7.8%). Housing stability captures financial resilience that income alone misses.

Risk is not driven by a single factor. It emerges from the interaction of baseline vulnerability, financial stress, and stability signals — with external scores capturing and compressing all of these dynamics into a single number.

Building the synthetic rejection population

Because the Home Credit dataset only includes approved applicants' outcomes, we constructed a synthetic rejection rule from the same risk factors the EDA identified. The result is an overall approval rate of 63.5%, with age-group approval rates ranging from 36.4% for borrowers in their 20s to 79.7% for those over 60. The approved group defaults at 5.1%; the rejected population's true rate is 13.3%. That 8.2 percentage-point gap is the selection distortion we measure and correct throughout the project.

Approval rate by age group. The approval filter is strongly age-dependent. Fewer than 4 in 10 applicants under 30 are approved, compared to nearly 8 in 10 borrowers over 60.
Approval rate by external score quintile. The filter operates heavily through external scores. Bottom-quintile applicants face only a 16.8% approval rate.
02
Phase 1

Baseline Models & The Size of the Problem

We trained logistic regression and XGBoost on approved applicants only, then tested on three populations: approved, full, and rejected. The results establish the baseline distortion that every subsequent phase works to correct.

3–4×ECE increase, approved → rejected
0.7032LR AUC on approved test
0.7114LR AUC on rejected test
94%Log-loss increase, approved → rejected
ModelTest PopulationAUCLog-LossECE
Logistic RegressionApproved0.70320.18570.0018
Logistic RegressionFull population0.74790.24910.0031
Logistic RegressionRejected0.71140.35960.0057
XGBoostApproved0.72140.18260.0024
XGBoostFull population0.76190.24510.0038
XGBoostRejected0.72810.35400.0063

AUC barely moves across populations — both models rank rejected applicants roughly as well as approved ones. Calibration error is the story. LR's ECE goes from 0.0018 on approved applicants to 0.0057 on rejected ones. XGBoost goes from 0.0024 to 0.0063. Both roughly triple. The model can rank borrowers correctly while being systematically wrong about how risky any individual actually is.

Calibration curves across all three test populations. Curves closer to the diagonal are better-calibrated. The separation between approved and rejected populations is consistent — real calibration degradation, not noise.

Selection bias in this dataset is a calibration problem, not a discrimination problem. AUC moves modestly. ECE moves sharply. A model can rank borrowers correctly while being systematically wrong about the actual probability of default for anyone it wasn't trained to see.

03
Phase 2

Three Ways to Fix It

01

Parcelling

Sort into risk bands, impute default labels from observed rates × 1.2× adjustment. Assumption-heavy; the adjustment factor is unverifiable without rejected outcomes.

02

Expectation-Maximization

Each rejected applicant becomes two weighted rows — one labeled default, one not — with weights equal to the current soft probability. Converges toward the true population structure without stochastic noise.

03

Inverse Probability Weighting

Never guesses labels. Upweights approved applicants who look like they could have been rejected. Clean assumptions; ESS drops to 36.8% of nominal (46,756 from 126,889).

Parcelling

In the lowest-risk band, approved applicants outnumber rejected 18,456 to 1,532. In the highest-risk band, that ratio inverts: 2,208 approved versus 17,781 rejected. The 1.2× adjustment overshoots the true rejected default rate slightly (imputed 14.9% vs actual 13.3%).

Risk band composition and default rates. The selection structure in visual form — approved applicants dominate low-risk bands, rejected applicants dominate high-risk bands. The 1.2× adjustment creates visible separation between observed and imputed curves.
Parcelling sensitivity across band counts and adjustment factors. AUC ranges only from 0.7430 to 0.7479 — essentially flat. ECE is highly sensitive to the adjustment factor: at 2.0× it degrades sharply. The correct value is unknowable without rejected outcomes.

EM: reaching the true population rate

After 100 iterations, the EM algorithm's final parameter change was 0.014. The final imputed default rate for rejected applicants was 13.31% — nearly identical to the true 13.32%. This is the EM's main accomplishment: without ever seeing rejected outcomes, it recovered the population-level default rate with near-perfect accuracy.

EM convergence across 100 iterations. Log-likelihood trends upward. The imputed rejected default rate converges toward 13.3%, matching truth. Parameter change stabilizes as the model settles.

IPW: reweighting without imputing

The propensity model (XGBoost) achieved an AUC of 0.9399 — the approval decisions are almost perfectly predictable from the feature set. The effective sample size after trimming at the 99th percentile is 46,756 (36.8% of nominal). The information cost of reweighting is real.

Propensity score distribution and resulting IPW weights. Most approved applicants receive weights near the median (0.668). A long right tail extends to the cap of 10.890, trimmed to prevent single observations from dominating the model.

How they compare

MethodAUC (full)AUC (rejected)ECE (full)ECE (rejected)Mean pred.
Baseline LR0.74790.71140.00310.00570.0803
Parcelling0.74700.71090.00470.00890.0854
EM0.74790.71130.00270.00720.0802
IPW0.74500.71250.00330.00780.0795
True rate————0.0807
Calibration comparison across all methods. EM achieves the tightest full-population calibration. IPW holds near baseline. Parcelling's 1.2× adjustment introduces visible overshoot.
Score distribution shift. EM lands closest to the true population rate (0.0807). Parcelling consistently overshoots at 0.0854.

No correction improved discrimination meaningfully — AUC moves by less than 0.003 across all methods. The approval filter operates entirely on features the model can already see, so it partially self-corrects without intervention. EM improves full-population ECE to 0.0027 — better than the baseline — while IPW delivers the best rejected-population AUC at 0.7125. Parcelling is the weakest method on both dimensions.

04
Phase 3

Not Just Whether — But When

Binary classification collapses time. A default in month 3 and a default in month 30 both get a label of 1 — but they represent different operational problems for provisioning, collections, and capital planning. Survival analysis recovers the timing dimension that binary models discard.

More importantly: the approved-only training set is systematically missing the fastest defaulters, because high-risk borrowers both default earlier and are rejected more aggressively. The verification output confirmed this precisely.

9 mo.Median time-to-default, approved defaulters
4 mo.Median time-to-default, rejected defaulters
5 mo.Temporal gap survival models must recover

We built three survival models — an approved-only Cox PH baseline, an IPW-reweighted Cox model, and a DeepHitSingle neural model — all using the top 25 features by XGBoost importance.

ModelC-IndexIBSIBS vs. null (0.25)
Cox Baseline (approved only)0.68070.03240.2176
Cox IPW-reweighted0.67990.03230.2177
DeepHitSingle (neural)0.68840.05540.1946

DeepHitSingle leads on C-index (0.6884 vs 0.6807) — a gap of 0.0077 over the approved-only Cox baseline, reflecting its ability to model non-proportional hazard relationships that Cox PH cannot capture by assumption. The two Cox models are essentially tied on C-index (0.6807 vs 0.6799), which makes sense — IPW reweighting shifts hazard estimates without changing the rank ordering that C-index measures.

On IBS, the picture reverses. Cox IPW achieves the best IBS (0.0323), narrowly beating the baseline (0.0324). DeepHitSingle's IBS (0.0554) is substantially higher — meaning it sacrifices calibration for discriminative power. The two metrics are measuring different things, and they point toward different model choices for different banking workflows.

Cumulative hazard curves: baseline vs. IPW-corrected Cox. IPW estimates higher early-period hazard (months 1–12), then converges with the baseline — the temporal correction in action. The approved-only model underestimated how quickly defaults accumulate in the first year.
Survival curves for three risk profiles. High-risk applicants accumulate most of their default probability in the first 12–18 months, consistent with the 4-month median for rejected defaulters.
Time-resolved Brier Score. IPW's calibration improvement concentrates in months 1–12, where selection distortion was strongest. The curves converge after the first year.
DeepHitSingle training curve. Training and validation loss converge cleanly. Early stopping triggered at epoch 11 with best validation loss of 0.1349 at lr=0.001.

Survival analysis surfaces a distortion that binary classification cannot see: the approved-only model underestimates early-period default hazard because the fastest defaulters were disproportionately rejected. For ranking, use DeepHitSingle (C-index 0.6884). For calibration and provisioning, use Cox IPW (IBS 0.0323). No single model wins both.

05
Phase 4

Bias, Variance, and Why Simple Models Hold Up

The bias-variance analysis confirmed the modeling choices made throughout and surfaced one finding worth dwelling on: logistic regression's simplicity turns out to be a form of robustness under population shift.

XGBoost shows a conventional positive overfitting gap — training AUC 0.7725 versus validation AUC 0.7560, a gap of 0.0165. Logistic regression shows a negative overfitting gap of -0.0315. Its validation AUC (0.7445) is higher than its training AUC (0.7130). This is structural, not surprising: the validation set includes rejected applicants with a 13.3% default rate, making discrimination easier. And LR's linear boundary simply cannot memorize approval-specific patterns that would fail on rejected applicants.

Learning curves. LR's negative gap reflects the easier validation set and its inability to overfit approval-specific patterns. XGBoost's 0.0165 gap is well-controlled and stable across training sizes.
Regularization sensitivity. Performance plateaus quickly after C=0.1, confirming the cross-validation selection. The bottleneck is model form, not coefficient magnitude.
XGBoost depth sensitivity. Depth=4 provides the best validation AUC. Depths 6 and 8 widen the overfitting gap substantially without meaningful validation improvement.
DeepHitSingle learning rate sweep. lr=0.001 achieves the best validation loss (0.1344) at epoch 22. Lower rates converge to a similar solution with more iterations. Higher rates overshoot early.
Calibration: approved vs. full population test sets. All models calibrate well on approved applicants. On the full population, IPW-corrected LR sits closest to the true rate (0.0807).

Logistic regression's underfitting is partially self-correcting under selection bias. Its inability to memorize approval-specific patterns means it generalizes more stably across populations. Use XGBoost for discrimination. Use logistic regression or IPW-corrected probabilities when the actual probability estimate matters for pricing, provisioning, or regulatory reporting.

06
Phase 5

Who Gets the Wrong Probability?

Aggregate metrics hide group-level disparities. We evaluated three demographic axes — gender, education, and age — measuring calibration error, mean predicted probability, false positive rate, and false negative rate under both the baseline and IPW-corrected models, all on the full-population test set including rejected applicants.

Per-group fairness diagnostics across gender, education, and age. IPW's corrective effect is concentrated on age — the axis most correlated with the approval filter. Gender and education disparities are largely unchanged.

Where IPW helps: age

The baseline model's ECE gap across age groups was 0.0099. IPW cuts that to 0.0054 — a 45% reduction. The largest single improvement is for borrowers under 30, whose ECE falls from 0.0139 to 0.0107. This is mechanistically consistent: age was the strongest input to the approval filter, so younger applicants were most over-represented in the rejected population. Fix the selection problem and you fix most of the age disparity, because they were the same problem.

Where IPW is neutral: gender and education

Gender ECE gap changes from 0.0073 to 0.0077 (+0.0004). Education ECE gap changes from 0.0188 to 0.0196 (+0.0008). Both within sampling noise. IPW didn't introduce new disparities on these axes either — it corrected the distortion it was designed to correct and left everything else alone.

The threshold problem

At a fixed population-level threshold, IPW actually widens the FNR gap on age from 0.4193 to 0.4589. The mechanism is specific: IPW pushes predicted probabilities upward for younger, higher-risk applicants, improving detection of young defaulters (under-30 FNR drops from 0.1741 to 0.1674) while worsening detection of older ones (60+ FNR rises from 0.5934 to 0.6262). The 60+ group suffers most because their true default rate (5.1%) already sits below the population threshold. Segment-specific thresholds or fairness constraints during training are needed to address this — probability recalibration alone is not sufficient.

IPW improves fairness where selection bias was the underlying cause of disparity, and stays out of the way elsewhere. For regulatory reporting, this is the ideal behavior — a targeted correction, not a blunt adjustment that introduces new problems. Threshold-based error rate disparity is a separate problem that requires separate treatment.

The Same Pattern, Five Times Over

Every phase of this project produced the same finding from a different angle. Selection bias in credit risk modeling is a calibration problem, not a discrimination problem. Its effects concentrate exactly where the training data was thinnest — and the correction methods that work best are the ones that respect that structure rather than filling in the gaps with assumptions.

ECE tripled to nearly 4× from approved to rejected populations (LR: 0.0018 → 0.0057; XGB: 0.0024 → 0.0063). AUC barely moved.

EM achieved ECE 0.0027 — better than the baseline — and recovered 13.31% vs. 13.32% true rejected default rate. No method improved AUC meaningfully.

DeepHitSingle leads on discrimination (C-index 0.6884). Cox IPW leads on calibration (IBS 0.0323). The 5-month temporal gap is measurable and partially recoverable.

LR's -0.0315 overfitting gap is self-correcting under population shift. XGBoost's 0.0165 gap is well-controlled. Bias is a stability property, not just a limitation.

IPW cut age ECE disparity by 45% (0.0099 → 0.0054). Gender and education were unchanged. Correction methods fix the disparities they're designed to fix — and no others.

A bank that backtests on historical approved loans will see good calibration and feel confident. The moment it expands its approval policy — taking on applicants who look like historically rejected ones — realized default rates will exceed predictions. Not uniformly. Most severely for the subgroups that were rejected most aggressively.

The fix isn't a better algorithm. It's knowing what the training data actually represents, and being honest about what it doesn't.

Lacy Dove  ·  Michael Beyer  ·  Aryan Sanan  ·  Harelle Keli
